category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Stochastic Processes — Markov Chains and Absorbing States

questiontext.text: A Markov chain on \(\{1,2\}\) has transition matrix \(P=\begin{pmatrix}0.7&0.3\\0.4&0.6\end{pmatrix}\). The stationary distribution \(\pi=(\pi_1,\pi_2)\) satisfies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\pi=(4/7,3/7)\)

Stationary: \(\pi P=\pi\). So \(0.7\pi_1+0.4\pi_2=\pi_1\Rightarrow0.4\pi_2=0.3\pi_1\Rightarrow\pi_1/\pi_2=4/3\). With \(\pi_1+\pi_2=1\): \(\pi_1=4/7,\pi_2=3/7\). For an ergodic chain with 2 states, stationary distribution has \(\pi_i=q_j/(q_j+q_i)\) where \(q_{12}=0.3\), \(q_{21}=0.4\): \(\pi_1=0.4/0.7=4/7\).

questiontext.text: A state \(i\) in a Markov chain is absorbing if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(p_{ii}=1\) (once entered, cannot leave)

An absorbing state has \(p_{ii}=1\) and \(p_{ij}=0\) for all \(j\neq i\). A Markov chain is absorbing if it has at least one absorbing state and every state can reach an absorbing state. In the canonical form, the transition matrix is partitioned into absorbing and transient states. Key quantities: absorption probabilities and expected absorption times.

answer.text: The state has the highest stationary probability

questiontext.text: In the Gambler's Ruin problem: a gambler starts at \$k, wins \$1 with probability \(p\) and loses \$1 with probability \(q=1-p\) per round, stopping at 0 (ruin) or \$N (success). For \(p\neq1/2\), the probability of ruin starting at \$k is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\dfrac{(q/p)^N-(q/p)^k}{(q/p)^N-1}\)

Gambler's ruin: let \(r=(q/p)\). Probability of ruin from state \(k\): \(P_k=\frac{r^N-r^k}{r^N-1}\) for \(p\neq1/2\). For \(p=1/2\): \(P_k=1-k/N\). As \(N\to\infty\) (unlimited wealth): \(P_k=\min(1,(q/p)^k)\) — ruin is certain if \(p\leq1/2\).

questiontext.text: A Markov chain is irreducible if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Every state is reachable from every other state

Irreducible: all states communicate (i.e., for any pair \(i,j\), \(\exists n\) with \(p_{ij}^{(n)}>0\) and \(\exists m\) with \(p_{ji}^{(m)}>0\)). An irreducible chain with a finite state space has a unique stationary distribution. Irreducible + aperiodic implies the chain converges to stationary distribution from any starting state.

answer.text: It has a unique absorbing state
Every state is reachable from every other state
It has at least one transient state

questiontext.text: The period of state \(i\) is \(d(i)=\gcd\{n\geq1:p_{ii}^{(n)}>0\}\). A state with period 1 is called:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Aperiodic

Aperiodic: \(d(i)=1\). Aperiodic states can be revisited in non-regular intervals. A finite irreducible aperiodic chain has a unique stationary distribution and converges to it: \(p_{ij}^{(n)}\to\pi_j\) as \(n\to\infty\). Periodic chains (e.g., bipartite graphs have period 2) exhibit oscillatory behavior and do not converge.

questiontext.text: For an irreducible positive recurrent Markov chain with stationary distribution \(\pi\), the expected return time to state \(i\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(1/\pi_i\)

Mean recurrence time: \(m_i=E[\text{first return time to }i]=1/\pi_i\). Positive recurrent: \(m_i<\infty\). Null recurrent: \(m_i=\infty\) (but still recurrent, \(\pi_i=0\)). Transient: \(P(\text{return to }i)<1\). For finite irreducible chains: all states are positive recurrent.

question.single: truetrueabc \(\pi_i\) \(n\pi_i\) \(1/\pi_i\) Depends on the starting state

questiontext.text: For an absorbing Markov chain with transition matrix \(P=\begin{pmatrix}Q&R\\0&I\end{pmatrix}\) (Q=transient-to-transient, R=transient-to-absorbing), the fundamental matrix is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(N=(I-Q)^{-1}\)

Fundamental matrix \(N=(I-Q)^{-1}\). Entry \(N_{ij}\) = expected number of times the chain is in transient state \(j\) starting from transient state \(i\) before absorption. Absorption probabilities: \(B=NR\). Expected absorption time from state \(i\): \(t_i=\sum_j N_{ij}\) (row sum of \(N\)).

questiontext.text: A Markov chain on \(\{1,2,3,4,5\}\) has absorbing states \(\{1,5\}\). Starting from state 3, \(\lim_{n\to\infty}P(X_n=1)\) represents:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) The probability of being absorbed into state 1 starting from state 3

For absorbing chains, \(P^n\to\begin{pmatrix}I&0\\B&0\end{pmatrix}\) where \(B=NR\) gives the absorption probabilities. \(\lim_{n\to\infty}P(X_n=1|X_0=3)=B_{3,1}\), the \((3,1)\) entry of \(B\). The chain eventually gets absorbed into either state 1 or state 5 with these computed probabilities.

question.single: truetrueabc The probability of being absorbed into state 1 starting from state 3 The stationary probability of state 1 0 always The transition probability \(p_{31}\)

questiontext.text: State \(i\) is recurrent if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\sum_{n=0}^\infty p_{ii}^{(n)}=\infty\)

Foster's criterion: \(i\) is recurrent iff \(\sum_{n=0}^\infty p_{ii}^{(n)}=\infty\) (expected number of returns to \(i\) is infinite). Transient iff this sum is finite. Equivalently: \(P(\text{ever return to }i\mid X_0=i)=1\) iff recurrent. For irreducible chains: all states are either all recurrent or all transient.

questiontext.text: The ergodic theorem for Markov chains states: for an irreducible positive recurrent aperiodic chain,

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(p_{ij}^{(n)}\to\pi_j\) as \(n\to\infty\) for all \(i,j\)

Ergodic theorem: for any starting state \(i\), the \(n\)-step transition probability converges to the unique stationary distribution \(\pi_j\), regardless of starting state. This requires: irreducible + positive recurrent + aperiodic. The time-average also converges: \(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\mathbf{1}[X_k=j]\to\pi_j\) a.s.

answer.text: \(p_{ij}^{(n)}\to1/N\) for all \(i,j\) where \(N\) is the number of states

questiontext.text: The Chapman–Kolmogorov equations state: \(p_{ij}^{(m+n)}=\)

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\sum_k p_{ik}^{(m)}p_{kj}^{(n)}\)

Chapman–Kolmogorov: \(P^{(m+n)}=P^{(m)}\cdot P^{(n)}\), i.e., \(p_{ij}^{(m+n)}=\sum_k p_{ik}^{(m)}p_{kj}^{(n)}\). The \(n\)-step matrix is the \(n\)-th power of the 1-step matrix: \(P^{(n)}=P^n\). This is the fundamental property that makes Markov chain analysis tractable.

questiontext.text: A Markov chain satisfies detailed balance with respect to \(\pi\) if \(\pi_i p_{ij}=\pi_j p_{ji}\) for all \(i,j\). This implies:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\pi\) is a stationary distribution (the chain is reversible)

Detailed balance (reversibility): \(\pi_i p_{ij}=\pi_j p_{ji}\). Summing over \(i\): \(\sum_i\pi_i p_{ij}=\pi_j\sum_i p_{ji}=\pi_j\). So \(\pi\) is stationary. A chain satisfying detailed balance is called reversible (time-reversed process has same distribution). Not all chains are reversible (e.g., random walk on a directed cycle).

questiontext.text: For a birth-death chain with birth rates \(\lambda_i\) and death rates \(\mu_i\), the stationary distribution satisfies (local balance):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\pi_i\lambda_i=\pi_{i+1}\mu_{i+1}\)

Local (detailed) balance for birth-death chains: \(\pi_i\lambda_i=\pi_{i+1}\mu_{i+1}\). This gives the stationary distribution: \(\pi_k=\pi_0\prod_{i=1}^k\frac{\lambda_{i-1}}{\mu_i}\). For M/M/1 queue: \(\lambda_i=\lambda,\mu_i=\mu\), giving \(\pi_k=\rho^k(1-\rho)\) where \(\rho=\lambda/\mu<1\) for stability.

questiontext.text: The mixing time of a Markov chain measures:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) How quickly the distribution converges to stationarity

Mixing time \(t_{mix}(\epsilon)=\min\{t:\max_i\|P^t(i,\cdot)-\pi\|_{TV}\leq\epsilon\}\). It depends on the spectral gap (difference between the two largest eigenvalues of \(P\)): faster mixing \(\Leftrightarrow\) larger spectral gap. Important in MCMC algorithms (how long to run before samples are approximately from the stationary distribution).

answer.text: How quickly the chain's distribution converges to the stationary distribution

name.text: Markov - Random Walk on Z Recurrence

questiontext.text: The simple symmetric random walk on \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) (2D) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Recurrent (returns to origin with probability 1)

Pólya's theorem: simple symmetric random walk is recurrent in dimensions 1 and 2, and transient in dimension \(\geq3\). In 2D: \(\sum_n p_{00}^{(2n)}=\infty\), so it is null-recurrent (returns a.s. but expected return time is \(\infty\)). In 1D: positive-recurrent for finite state spaces; null-recurrent for \(\mathbb{Z}\).

answer.text: Null recurrent (returns a.s. but mean return time \(=\infty\))

questiontext.text: For an absorbing Markov chain, \(\lim_{n\to\infty}P^n_{ij}\) where \(j\) is a transient state equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) 0

For a transient state \(j\): \(\sum_n p_{jj}^{(n)}<\infty\), so \(p_{ij}^{(n)}\to0\). The chain eventually leaves all transient states and gets absorbed. The limiting distribution concentrates on absorbing states: \(\lim_{n\to\infty}P^n_{ij}=0\) for transient \(j\), and \(\lim P^n_{ij}=b_{ij}\) (absorption probability into \(j\)) for absorbing \(j\).

questiontext.text: For the 2-state chain with \(P=\begin{pmatrix}1-a&a\\b&1-b\end{pmatrix}\), \(P^n_{11}=\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\dfrac{b}{a+b}+\dfrac{a}{a+b}(1-a-b)^n\)

For a 2-state ergodic chain: the eigenvalues are 1 and \(1-a-b\). \(P^n_{11}=\pi_1+(1-\pi_1)(1-a-b)^n\) where \(\pi_1=b/(a+b)\). As \(n\to\infty\): \(P^n_{11}\to\pi_1=b/(a+b)\). The convergence rate is \(|1-a-b|<1\) (for \(a+b<2\)).

questiontext.text: A stochastic matrix (transition matrix) \(P\) satisfies: all entries \(\geq0\) and:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Each row sums to 1

A stochastic matrix: \(p_{ij}\geq0\) and \(\sum_j p_{ij}=1\) for each row \(i\) (the chain must go somewhere from each state). This ensures 1 is always an eigenvalue (with left eigenvector \(\mathbf{1}=(1,1,\ldots,1)^T\) and right eigenvector the stationary distribution). Doubly stochastic: rows AND columns sum to 1 (uniform distribution is stationary).

questiontext.text: In a Markov chain, states \(i\) and \(j\) communicate (\(i\leftrightarrow j\)) if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(i\) can reach \(j\) and \(j\) can reach \(i\)

Communication is an equivalence relation partitioning the state space into communicating classes. Within a class: all states are simultaneously recurrent or transient. Closed classes contain no path out. An irreducible chain has only one communicating class (all states). The structure of a Markov chain is determined by its communicating class structure.

answer.text: \(i\) can reach \(j\) AND \(j\) can reach \(i\) (both \(\exists\) paths)

questiontext.text: A Markov chain on \(\{0,1,2,3\}\) has 0 and 3 as absorbing states and the transition matrix for transient states \(\{1,2\}\) is \(Q=\begin{pmatrix}1/2&1/4\\1/4&1/2\end{pmatrix}\). The fundamental matrix \(N=(I-Q)^{-1}\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\begin{pmatrix}8/3&4/3\\4/3&8/3\end{pmatrix}\)

\(I-Q=\begin{pmatrix}1/2&-1/4\\-1/4&1/2\end{pmatrix}\). \(\det(I-Q)=1/4-1/16=3/16\). \((I-Q)^{-1}=\frac{1}{3/16}\begin{pmatrix}1/2&1/4\\1/4&1/2\end{pmatrix}=\frac{16}{3}\begin{pmatrix}1/2&1/4\\1/4&1/2\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}8/3&4/3\\4/3&8/3\end{pmatrix}\).
